title_en: A Luxurious Way to Use a Wood-Burning Stove
excerpt_en: 6/23 I spent the morning working online. At lunchtime, through an introduction from GPS runner Naoki Shimizu, who I'm grateful to back in Japan, I had an online chat with someone from Shimane Prefecture. This person researches Buddhism and psychology,
body_en:
6/23

I spent the morning working online. At lunchtime, through an introduction from GPS runner Naoki Shimizu, who I'm grateful to back in Japan,

I had an online chat with someone from Shimane Prefecture.

This person researches Buddhism and psychology, and we exchanged thoughts for about 40 minutes on topics like AI, my activities, and each other's work.

It's not something that leads to business right away, but I'm grateful that a new connection was made like this.

Being able to talk online with people in Japan while living overseas really shows what a great era we live in.

The time difference between Japan and Ireland is 8 hours, so I schedule my meetings at lunchtime to match the evening hours for people in Japan. That way, even while staying at the castle, I keep up my interactions with folks back home.

A 3km One-Way Trip to the Supermarket

In the afternoon, I headed out to the town of Tullamore for a grocery run. Charleville Castle, where I live, is nestled in the woods, and the supermarket is about 3km away—roughly a 40-minute walk one way.

The purpose of this shopping trip was to get ingredients to try out a new cooking method, since the microwave had broken down.

I actually came up with the idea of cooking on the wood-burning stove, so I bought potatoes, eggplant, chicken, pork, and garlic.

I figured if I wrapped everything in aluminum foil and roasted it in the stove, I could make things like butter potatoes and grilled eggplant.

I bought so many ingredients that my bag was stuffed to the brim, and the walk home was pretty brutal. With no car or bicycle, it looks like I'll be walking to the store for groceries as long as I'm living at the castle.

Taking on Cooking with the Wood-Burning Stove!

Once I got back to the castle, I started prepping right away. **I asked ChatGPT, "How do you cook with a wood-burning stove?" and got some advice. I made some simple preparations—scoring the eggplant, putting butter and soy sauce on the potatoes, seasoning the chicken before wrapping everything in aluminum foil.

Then I lit the stove and once it was hot, I put the food in. Since this was my first attempt, I had no idea how long things would take to cook, so I used ChatGPT's suggested 20–25 minutes as a guide.

The result… the potatoes and eggplant turned out perfectly! The eggplant in particular was amazing—I'd added finely chopped garlic, butter, and soy sauce, and it melted in my mouth. I never imagined I'd be eating such delicious grilled eggplant in an old Irish castle. Ha!

The potatoes came out fluffy and perfect as butter potatoes too. Totally satisfying.

However, I overcooked the pork and it burned to a crisp. I could still eat it, but the result was a bit disappointing.

Survival Living Sparks New Challenges

Losing the microwave—a precious cooking appliance—forced me to rack my brain about how to still eat well.

And that ultimately led me to a brand new challenge: "cooking"**—something I'd barely ever done in my life.

I'm not sure if you can even call this proper cooking, but

I discovered that the wood-burning stove makes roasted food taste absolutely incredible,

so I want to keep experimenting with different things.

Going forward, I want to master the art of roasting the best ingredients to boost my survival skills.

This is truly a valuable experience for me.

I'm going to keep raising the quality

and taking on more wood-burning stove cooking challenges! Haha

excerpt_en: June 24 Lately, as an international volunteer at the castle, I've been working on renovating the mobile home (trailer house) located in the castle's annex area. It seems like many volunteers used to live here, but now nobody uses it,
body_en:
June 24

Lately, as an international volunteer at the castle, I've been working on renovating the mobile home (trailer house) located in the castle's annex area.

It seems like many volunteers used to live here, but now nobody uses it, and the place is really run-down and dirty.

My mission is to clean up this mobile home. As preparation for welcoming many more international volunteers in the future, I want to get it into a comfortable state for them while I'm still living here.

From Outside to Inside: Painstaking Work Amid Dust and Debris

Over the past few days, I had been dismantling the wooden structures on the outside of the trailer house, but starting today, I decided to tackle the interior cleaning.

I've gotten pretty good with the axe by now! Ha!

The inside is full of garbage and dust, with holes in the ceiling and broken windows in some places.

Today, I focused on throwing out and sorting through all of this junk.

The dust is so bad that goggles and a mask are essential.

I'm working with protective gear to avoid itchy eyes and inhaling debris.

First, I gathered up all the trash in the rooms to make it easier to walk around. Clothes that were in the dressers I bundled up and set aside for now. Unfortunately, it was raining today, so I couldn't hang them outside to dry—I'll do that on a sunny day.

Traces Left Behind by Past Volunteers

Next, I cleared away some really gnarly spider webs. When I moved to the next room, it was also full of junk—scattered playing cards and piles of things I couldn't even identify. Most likely, the volunteers who lived here before just left everything behind when they moved out.

As a Japanese person, I think it's only natural to clean up the place you've lived in before you leave. I plan to leave this place cleaner than I found it when my stay is over.

Running the Washing Machine and Contributing Even More

While I was working, I got a message from the castle saying, "There are a lot of clothes here, could you sort them out?"

They wanted me to separate clothes that could be donated from ones too dirty to give away, so I got right on it.

I gathered up all the clothes that still looked usable and decided to run them through the washing machine. Nobody specifically asked me to do this, but I thought it was one more way I could contribute. I figured they should be clean and ready to donate at any time.

Building a Long-Term Relationship as a Volunteer

When you think of castle volunteering, you might imagine big projects like building renovations, but in reality, the work is much more basic than that.

My stay is for one month, but I want to build the kind of relationship where I can come back and volunteer here again whenever I return to Ireland.

To make that happen, I believe it's important to contribute more than what's expected of me.

Right now, my PC is updating and charging, so work has temporarily paused. I'm going to use this time to focus on my volunteer activities.

title_en: Hands-On Manual Labor Meets Cutting-Edge AI Technology
excerpt_en: 6/25 Today was day two of cleaning inside the trailer house. I started by carrying all the stuff from inside out and airing the place out. Since I'd already finished sorting the clothes by yesterday, I hung those outside to dry,
body_en:
6/25

Today was day two of cleaning inside the trailer house. I started by carrying all the stuff from inside out and airing the place out.

Since I'd already finished sorting the clothes by yesterday, I hung those outside to dry, brought the other items out temporarily, and made sure to thoroughly ventilate and dry everything.

The weather in Ireland is unpredictable, and this past week has been mostly cloudy or rainy.

Luckily, today's forecast called for half a day without rain, so I pushed through as much work as possible during that window. I used brooms and brushes to thoroughly clean up the interior, which had been covered in dust and spider webs.

This isn't the kind of work you can finish in a single day—it requires slow, steady progress.

It's not the flashy kind of volunteering like renovating a large building, but I feel that diligently cleaning up the small, overlooked areas that nobody else wants to deal with is exactly how I can contribute.

My mission is to make sure this place is comfortable and ready for the next volunteer who arrives.

Wood-Burning Stove Cooking, Round Two!

Now, switching topics—I've been continuing to cook on the wood-burning stove. Today, I took another crack at the pork that I burned last time.

This time, before wrapping it in aluminum foil, I seasoned it thoroughly with butter, chopped garlic, and soy sauce.

I also tried cooking chicken the same way for the first time. Since I overcooked things last time, I shortened the cooking time and held it over the stove fire for about 15 minutes.

The result… a huge success!

Everything was perfectly cooked through with no burning whatsoever—absolutely ideal. It looked so delicious that I couldn't even wait to sit down—I just stood there and devoured it.

The chicken roasted with soy sauce was amazing too. I couldn't believe how good it tasted with just simple seasoning on quality ingredients. And of course, my go-to grilled eggplant and butter potatoes were fantastic as always.

I never would have guessed that the microwave breaking down would lead me to discover the joy and depth of cooking.

This survival cooking, made possible only because I'm living in an ancient Irish castle, is an invaluable experience in my life. I want to keep trying different ingredients and mastering how to roast them. If I ever go back to Japan and end up living in the countryside, I'm sure this experience will come in handy.

Attempting to Update the OS on My 9-Year-Old MacBook

Over the past few days, I've had another big challenge on my hands: updating the OS on my beloved 9-year-old MacBook.

I wanted to try **"AI coding"—using AI to write programs—but my OS was too old to support it.

The update to the latest OS took a whopping 48 hours**. It felt like time had stopped, but thanks to that, I managed to get the latest OS running on this old machine.

Right away, I tried coding with an AI called "Claude." After tinkering with it for a few hours, I can say—this is seriously fun!

You can build simple websites and apps just by giving the AI instructions. I'm convinced this will be an essential skill for the future, so I plan to keep learning it seriously from here on.

Hands-on manual labor and cutting-edge AI technology. At first glance, they seem like polar opposites, but life at this castle gives me the time to immerse myself deeply in both.

Today was another jam-packed, fulfilling day. Within this limited time,

I want to keep tackling as much as I can.

excerpt_en: Now, there are two famous things in Tullamore. One is the castle where I live, and the other is Tullamore Dew whiskey. The reason I came to Ireland, as I wrote in Ireland Activity Report Part 1, is to collaborate with Irish companies.
body_en:
Now, there are two famous things in Tullamore.

One is the castle where I live,

and the other is Tullamore Dew whiskey.

The reason I came to Ireland,

as I wrote in Ireland Activity Report Part 1,

is to collaborate with Irish companies.

Whiskey and masu go incredibly well together,

so I'd love to collaborate in the future.

For that reason, I've already visited many whiskey distilleries across Ireland.

I actually didn't know there was a whiskey distillery in Tullamore

until it was decided that I'd be staying at the castle,

but that's gotta be fate.

So off I went!

For the full details, check out my note article below.

[Experience Report] Tullamore Dew Distillery, Ireland | A Journey into Whiskey and Craftsmanship | MaSU

Ireland—the holy land of Irish whiskey.

Among its many distilleries, I visited the one in Tullamore, known for its exceptionally well-curated tours.

Over about 2 hours, I learned about the whiskey-making process and took my time with the tasting.

It wasn't cheap, but the experience was worth more than the price. It planted a new ambition inside me.

And someday—

a collaboration between my brand "FOMUS" and this distillery's whiskey. I had a gut feeling it would be something truly amazing.

But I didn't go up to them and say "Let's collab" on the spot. I felt that would be reckless and disrespectful.

It's been nearly a year since I came to Ireland.

I have a strong desire to accomplish something in this land. But rushing things is a no-go. Especially in the world of whiskey, where history and tradition are deeply respected, trust is the foundation of everything.

"By partnering with FOMUS, here's what's in it for you."—I need to prepare materials that clearly present that value, and build the relationship over time. That's the path I've chosen.

One step at a time, steadily.

The road isn't easy. For a small brand like mine to become an equal partner with a historic distillery, first I myself—and my brand—need to earn solid credibility in this land of Ireland. It's not something money alone can solve.

Immerse yourself in the local culture and connect deeply with the people.

If that means walking 4km for an hour, I won't hesitate.

This distillery visit marks a new beginning in my journey.

For now, I've merely taken the first step toward a grand dream.

title_en: Will AI Really Take Our Jobs?
excerpt_en: 6/27 Will AI really take our jobs? My AI revolution that started with a 550,000-yen cost savings, and an investment in the future. While I'm deeply rooted in analog culture like the traditional masu, at the same time, cutting-edge AI technology
body_en:
6/27

Will AI really take our jobs? My AI revolution that started with a 550,000-yen cost savings, and an investment in the future.

While I'm deeply rooted in analog culture like the traditional masu, at the same time, cutting-edge AI technology has become the powerful engine driving my activities forward.

It might seem contradictory at first glance, but the fusion of these two elements is at the very core of the future I envision.

A Shocking Encounter with AI: "Midjourney" Broke Through the 550,000-Yen Barrier

Three years ago, I was blown away by the emergence of the image-generation AI "Midjourney." At the time, I had asked a professional illustrator for a quote to create visuals expressing the world of my character brand "KUKU." The price they quoted? A whopping 550,000 yen. Of course, the quality would have been worth it, but it was still a huge investment at the time.

But when I tried Midjourney, my entire perspective was turned upside down. Being able to generate visuals close to what I imagined, at essentially zero cost, and in unlimited quantities—I saw enormous potential. I could drastically cut the time and expense of hiring an illustrator while being able to experiment to my heart's content.

This was truly a "revolution" in my creative work.

AI Isn't a Threat—It's the Ultimate Weapon

Since that experience, my creative workflow has changed dramatically. In design and music production, 80–90% of my creative work has been replaced by AI.

Of course, the final fine-tuning and the sensory aspects still require a human touch, but AI now handles the foundational work and the trial-and-error process. This has freed up more time and energy for idea generation and strategic thinking.

And right now, what I'm most passionate about is a revolutionary technology called "Vibes Coding."

This lets someone like me—with absolutely zero programming knowledge—build websites and systems simply by giving instructions in Japanese, with the AI automatically handling the construction. Just yesterday, using this technology, I managed to build a fairly large-scale system all by myself in just 4 hours. That kind of speed and efficiency was unthinkable before.

What "Vibes Coding" demonstrates goes beyond mere convenience. Just as everyone once had to learn how to use Excel and Word, I'm absolutely certain that a time will come when everyone uses AI as a matter of course.

It won't be a technology only accessible to certain specialists—it will become a universal tool available to everyone.

Investing in the Future Together with My Team: The Possibilities AI Opens Up

That's exactly why I feel so strongly about the importance of learning and taking action right "now," in this very moment.

Recently, I gifted a new MacBook to a member of my team with a clear mission: "Level up your Vibes Coding skills." Because there's a limit to what I can accomplish alone.

But if I gain even one teammate with strong AI skills, I can hand off projects to them, and as a team, we can take on bigger jobs and more complex challenges.

AI doesn't take away human jobs—rather, it expands human capabilities and serves as a powerful partner for opening up new possibilities.

In 2025, this idea might still feel like a vague future vision for most people. But in a few years, everyone will be using AI as a matter of course, and it will have become an indispensable tool in business and creative fields.

To establish a solid position in that inevitable future, I'm continuing to learn, to challenge myself, and to generously share what I've gained with my team, right here, right now.

This is what I consider the most reliable investment in the future. AI isn't just a tool—I believe it's the key to creating the future and expanding our possibilities infinitely.

title_en: Days Going Back and Forth Between the Castle and the Mobile House: A New Way of Working Opened Up by AI
excerpt_en: 6/28 My base of operations right now is the mobile house. While volunteering at the ancient castle in Tullamore, I spend my days in this little mobile base engaging with technology. A future beyond the cleanup Today I was busy with
body_en:
6/28

My base of operations right now is the mobile house.

While volunteering at the ancient castle in Tullamore, I spend my days in this little mobile base engaging with technology.

A future beyond the cleanup

Today I was busy with volunteer work at the castle from the morning.

In particular, I spent the morning organizing the lumber that had been left around the grounds where my mobile house is. It's unglamorous work—hauling it out and tidying things up—but there's a real sense of accomplishment when you see the space opening up.

In the afternoon, I was holed up inside the castle pretty much the whole time. What was I doing? Completely immersed in using AI.

Lately, my activity reports have been all about AI, and that's intentional. I've long had the goal of deeply exploring AI technology and mastering it. Right now is essentially my intensive training period.

The Impact of "Vibes Coding"—Controlling AI in Japanese

What I'm most obsessed with right now is a technology called "Vibes Coding."

This is a groundbreaking approach that allows someone like me—with absolutely zero programming knowledge—to have AI autonomously build websites and systems just by giving instructions in Japanese. Traditional software development required engineers to write complex code using specialized programming languages (strings of English and numbers). Vibes Coding turns that whole paradigm on its head.

For example, if I say in Japanese, "I want a web page with these features," the AI instantly interprets that and generates the necessary code.

Just yesterday, using Vibes Coding, I managed to build a fairly large-scale system all by myself in just a few hours. This is more than just efficiency gains.

Of course, there are still tricky parts,

but this truly represents the democratization of technology—a glimpse of a future where anyone can turn their ideas into reality. My MacBook is running nonstop right now, carrying out Vibes Coding instructions as we speak.

The "Hybrid Work" of Volunteering and AI Development

So my routine lately goes like this: during the day, I do physical volunteer work, and in between or during breaks, I dive into AI development. Once I hit a stopping point at the castle, I head back to the mobile house, check the systems the AI has built, and give it the next set of instructions. Then when the check is done, I head back to the castle for more volunteer work.

I find this cycle extremely comfortable and productive as a way of working.

If I just stared at a computer screen all day, honestly, I'd get bored, and it would wear me out physically too. Of course, there are times when I need to respond quickly to something, but if that's all I did, the volunteer work would fall behind.

That's why I prioritize doing the volunteer work—my main commitment—properly, while running AI-powered development on the side as a hybrid approach. Contributing to the local community while simultaneously creating new products with cutting-edge AI technology.

By balancing these two different activities, I'm making steady progress on projects like building systems for a performance brand and creating new support sites. This way of working—where manual labor meets intellectual creation, tradition meets innovation—is a great joy for me, and I plan to keep it going.

Steady volunteer work and boundless AI creativity—my daily life where these coexist is always full of new discoveries and challenges.

title_en: Castle Cleanup and AI in Action
excerpt_en: 6/29 Right now, I'm working hard on volunteer activities at the castle. Every day brings new discoveries in this historic place, and I'm having a truly fulfilling time. Castle Cleanup and AI in Action Today, let me tell you about the tidying up
body_en:
6/29

Right now, I'm working hard on volunteer activities at the castle. Every day brings new discoveries in this historic place, and I'm having a truly fulfilling time.

Castle Cleanup and AI in Action

Today, let me tell you about the tidying-up work I've been doing lately.

There's a mobile home—a temporary dwelling a short distance from the castle—where international volunteers used to stay. It's not being used anymore.

Over the past few weeks, I've been shooting videos, doing work, and organizing this space.

The toughest part was hauling out all the clothes, fabrics, and massive amounts of clothing that had been piled up here.

All of it is being sorted for charity donations.

Doing this work alone was honestly some serious heavy lifting.

Sweating through it, I sorted T-shirts, pants, sheets, towels, and every kind of fabric—one by one, carefully separating them and stuffing them into bags.

When I finally finished hauling everything out, I was filled with a real sense of accomplishment. This kind of unglamorous work is part of my volunteering too.

The work of an international volunteer is never the same twice—there are always new challenges. But seeing this place gradually getting cleaner is genuinely satisfying.

By late July, when I plan to temporarily leave, I want to have this place completely emptied out and properly organized.

That's the latest update on what I've been up to.

A Dual-Wielding Work Style with AI

I managed to finish packing up the goods all by myself! Though unfortunately, some stuff didn't quite fit, and since some of it is pretty dirty, I'm thinking I might just go ahead and dispose of it.

Making the best judgment calls is part of the volunteer work too.

So while I'm pouring myself into this physical volunteer work every day, there's actually another side to it. My MacBook is always running, and inside it, AI is building various systems for me. You could call it a "dual-wielding" work style.

I regularly push forward with volunteer work, and when that wraps up, I immediately head back to the mobile house to check the systems the AI has built. Once I'm done checking, I go back to the castle and resume volunteer work. I've been repeating this cycle nonstop.

This cycle feels extremely efficient for me—it's an ideal way of working that keeps me from getting bored.

If I just sat in front of a computer doing programming all day, I'd honestly get bored, and my body would start to feel it too.

Sure, it's nice to be able to react quickly when something comes up, but if that's all I did, the main volunteer work wouldn't get done.

So what I really focus on is doing the volunteer work—my primary responsibility—properly, while running AI-powered programming on the side.

Alongside castle cleaning and maintenance volunteer work, I'm having AI handle projects like building systems for a performance brand and creating support websites.

I take responsibility for the volunteer work right in front of me, while maximizing AI within the unique setting of this castle to power through creative work.

I want to keep up this fusion of analog and digital activities going forward.

AI is an irreplaceable partner that lets me balance my volunteer work and creative projects. I'd be happy if this unique way of working piqued your interest.

title_en: Why a Programming Newbie Like Me Is Taking on Website Development
excerpt_en: 6/30 It rained all day in Ireland. On days like this when I can't do volunteer work outside the castle, it's the perfect opportunity for me. I spent the entire day glued to my computer, immersed in a new challenge. The reason for the challenge: expanding
body_en:
6/30

It rained all day in Ireland. On days like this when I can't do volunteer work outside the castle, it's the perfect opportunity for me. I spent the entire day glued to my computer, immersed in a new challenge.

The Reason for the Challenge: Global Expansion and the Limits of a Handmade Website

Starting this summer, my base of operations will expand beyond Japan and Ireland to include yet another country.

As I gear up to take my activities global, I felt I absolutely needed a website with a quality I could be proud of.

Up until now, all my websites have been built entirely by hand. I'm attached to them, of course, but a site made by someone with no design knowledge doesn't exactly hold up under a professional's eye. To expand the brand further, I needed a stronger "face."

Not a Designer—Building with an AI Agent

But once again, I chose not to hire a professional designer. I deliberately chose to take it on myself. And my partner in this endeavor is the buzzworthy "AI agent."

I have absolutely zero programming experience—I can't even read code. But by giving the AI agent instructions like "I want it to look like this," the AI writes the code for me and builds the website. For me, this is both a website development project and practice for mastering cutting-edge AI technology.

I've already secured the server and domain.

In a castle room with nothing but the sound of rain, I build the site piece by piece through conversation with the AI. I've been at it since yesterday, but I can do it all day without getting bored—it's that engaging.

Investing in Skills for the Future: The Ability to Do Everything Yourself

This challenge isn't just about completing one website. The AI wave that started with image generation has now reached a much more practical realm with "AI agents." I'm certain this technology will be needed in all sorts of situations going forward. To be ready for that, I need to become more proficient with it than anyone else.

I won't spare any time when it comes to learning new knowledge and skills. Aiming for completion by early July, I plan to make the most of my precious time at this castle to sharpen my skills.

Solo, focused work in a rainy old castle.

This is the best investment

I can make in my future self.

title_en: The Fusion of Evolving Web Development and Volunteer Work
excerpt_en: 7/1 Today I spent another full day in dialogue with AI. Rather than just working, it felt more like learning a new language—I focused on deepening my communication with AI. Today in particular, I spent almost the entire day on Vibes Coding.
body_en:
7/1

Today I spent another full day in dialogue with AI. Rather than just working, it felt more like learning a new language—I focused on deepening my communication with AI.

Today in particular, I spent almost the entire day on "Vibes Coding."

An Unquenchable Curiosity for AI and the Limits of "Claude Code"

I actually pulled an all-nighter last night.

Driven by the strong desire to master Vibes Coding as quickly as possible and use it freely, I sacrificed sleep to stay immersed in it.

I'm currently using a service called "Claude Code." I started on the $20/month plan, but I hit the limit astonishingly fast and honestly felt it wasn't enough.

So just one day later, I upgraded to the $100 plan. There's a top-tier $200 plan too, but I figured I wouldn't need that much. Still, even on the $100 plan, if I use it day after day, I regularly hit the limit.

It seems to reset once every 5 hours, so while working with AI, I sometimes get a message saying "You can't use this for 2 more hours."

When that happens, I put those "wait times" to good use. During the 2 hours I can't use AI, I head to the castle and do volunteer work.

Then, when AI access opens back up, I rush back and resume development. This cycle has become my current way of working.

The Possibilities AI Opens Up for Web Service Development

What I'm working on with AI's help is mainly website construction.

Starting with the official site for my performance brand, I've also been working on several other websites—ideas that came to me during consulting, like "I wish something like this existed!"

I feel like these could potentially grow into a significant business down the line.

And on top of that, I'm developing a brand new web

service that leverages AI.

When I realized this web service could potentially become a monetizable business, I started developing it using Vibes Coding, and that's where I am now.

The Joy of Creating from Zero Knowledge

Things that were once impossible without specialized engineers and programmers are different now. Even someone like me with zero programming knowledge can turn ideas and imagination into concrete reality using AI.

Since Vibes Coding only emerged very recently, I feel incredibly fortunate to be focusing on this technology at exactly the right time.

Contributing to the community through volunteer work while leveling up my AI skills in between.

I'm truly grateful for this setup. The castle owner has been generous enough to say that volunteer work isn't on a strict schedule—I can just pitch in during my free time. So I deliver concentrated volunteer results in short bursts and devote all the remaining time to AI training.

This way of working feels incredibly comfortable and fulfilling for me. The range of things I can do has expanded enormously.

It's only been 3 or 4 days since I dove into Vibes Coding, but my progress has been remarkable.

Before, building one site on a single screen was all I could handle, but now I'm working on 3 or 4 sites simultaneously. In just a few days, my efficiency has tripled or quadrupled.

Website construction that would normally cost hundreds of thousands or even millions of yen can now be done for just tens of thousands—combining my labor plus minimal costs like API fees and server charges (and nothing if you don't count my own labor). It's astounding how convenient things have become.

A big part of it is the environment: I can focus on work with almost no human interaction, and unlike my usual hostel life (often in dorms where someone's always around), here at the castle I have a private room where nobody interrupts me, so I can dive deep into my work.

I don't think there's a better place anywhere for AI learning and development.

I want to keep pursuing this new way of working and continue evolving alongside AI.

title_en: The Changes That Come from Not Seeing Anyone
excerpt_en: Another day spent immersed in dialogue with AI and volunteer work at the castle. Honestly, during this period I've dubbed "Overseas Activities Part 4," my days aren't exactly filled with dramatic changes. The routine days, finding blog topics
body_en:
Another day spent immersed in dialogue with AI and volunteer work at the castle.

Honestly, during this period I've dubbed "Overseas Activities Part 4," my days aren't exactly filled with dramatic changes.

Within this routine, I sometimes find it a bit tough to come up with blog topics. My activities are pretty much the same every day, and I rarely go out.

I occasionally walk to the nearby supermarket for groceries (an 80-minute round trip, ha!),

but even that doesn't really make for a blog post.

A Volunteer's Daily Life Where Horses Roam

Hearing all this, it might sound like I'm living a perfectly ordinary life, but for me, it's an extremely unique environment. I spend my days volunteering at a castle while focusing on AI coding.

What stood out today was seeing horses being let out to roam on the castle grounds. It seems to happen regularly—there are always several horses around the castle.

They were being taken for a walk, which temporarily blocked off the areas we usually pass through, and the horses were strolling around a wider range than usual. Watching a horse leisurely walk right up near the mobile house where I'm doing my cleanup work had this indescribable peacefulness to it.

They might have been out for their health, but watching them amble around so leisurely really put my mind at ease.

Coexisting with Nature and Reflecting Inward

Having horses this close in daily life is a fresh experience for me. It's not unusual to hear sheep bleating all around either. This is truly a place where you can feel what it means to live surrounded by rich nature.

For someone like me who hasn't had much experience with rural living, this lifestyle is incredibly precious, and I feel deep gratitude for it. It makes me realize that what I need right now is exactly this—time surrounded by nature.

As for human interaction, it's become quite rare lately. The only faces around are the ones I already know, and there are hardly any new encounters.

Along with that, I've stopped caring about my appearance. My beard is growing wild, and I've basically stopped styling my hair.

Most of the time, I'm wearing a hat. When I know I'll be meeting someone, I shave and tidy up, but normally I just wear glasses and skip the contact lenses entirely.

From Appearance to Inner Self: Shifting Values

That said, I never skip my skincare routine. I wash my face every day and religiously use toner and moisturizer.

Because the last thing I want is for someone to say I've gotten fat or my skin has gone downhill and that I "don't match the masu anymore." It's not about trying to look young—I just want to stay youthful and keep a clean image.

Still, I'll admit it honestly feels like a waste of time to shave and groom myself when I'm not seeing anyone.

This might be a departure from the address-hopper lifestyle I maintained overseas for more than 3 years.

Back when I was constantly meeting new people, I was extremely particular about my visuals and grooming—I was thorough about it. But if there's one thing that's changed since I started living at this castle, it might be that my awareness of personal grooming has faded.

Even after coming to Ireland, I had occasions to meet local models for masu photo shoots, so I was still conscious of my appearance then. But at the castle, there's nothing like that.

I don't meet photographers, and right now I'm basically just a guy sitting here face-to-face with AI.

I don't know how this lifestyle will change going forward, but I want to keep diving deep into AI and continuing my volunteer work just like this.
